sections.label: USER PROMPT
sections.content:
The code executed 7 actions in the previous sequence.
Executed actions: RIGHT, RIGHT, RIGHT, RIGHT, RIGHT, RIGHT, RIGHT.
You are still on the same level.
The game is over.
Current state: step 128, level 2.
Valid actions right now: UP, DOWN, LEFT, RIGHT, SPACE, MOUSE, ACTION7.
Only tool: `python`. It receives `current_frame`, `previous_frame`, `history`, `transitions`, `last_transition`, `valid_actions`, `last_action_result`, and `action(actions)`. It also provides `last_animation` (per-action frames of your last `action(...)` call) and `frame_stats` (how much this game has animated so far) -- optional; use them only if you judge the intermediate motion informative rather than incidental.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current world model from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE WORLD MODEL.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `level_completed`, or `done`.
end of world model. 
Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
If you include assistant text before a tool call, keep it short and use it to update the world model. Helpful optional prefixes are `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Action model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, and `Cross-level notes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
If you use MOUSE, include integer row and col arguments.
EFFICIENCY BUDGET — your score on each level is (baseline_actions / your_actions)^2, so every wasted action costs you quadratically.
Level 2: you have used 77 actions; a strong score needs about 50 or fewer. You are 1.5x over the target.
NET-ZERO WASTE: your last 77 actions returned the board to a state already seen 77 actions ago — that round-trip made no progress yet still cost 77 actions. Do not extend-and-retract or oscillate; a sequence that ends where it began is pure waste.
If you are not steadily making progress toward the level goal, commit to your single best hypothesis and the shortest sequence that tests it — do not exhaustively scan rows/columns or enumerate every option. Test one idea, read the result, then decide.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
The game is over (game_over=True), but the level wasn't completed. The current state shows:
- Dark gray (c): pixels=72, bbox=[18,15]-[29,29] - this is the dark gray character on the left side
- Black (B): pixels=64, bbox=[18,45]-[29,59] - this is the black character on the right side
- Yellow L (Y): pixels=72, bbox=[42,3]-[53,17] - this is the yellow L-shape on the bottom left
The dark gray and black characters swapped sides! The dark gray is now at [18,15]-[29,29] (left side), and the black is at [18,45]-[29,59] (right side).
The yellow L-shape is at [42,3]-[53,17] (bottom left).
So the current state is:
- Dark gray at [18,15]-[29,29] (top left)
- Black at [18,45]-[29,59] (top right)
- Yellow L at [42,3]-[53,17] (bottom left)
This looks like the initial state of Level 2, but with the dark gray and black characters swapped! In the initial state, the dark gray was on the right and the black was on the left (or they were overlapping).
Wait, the game is over, so I can't take any more actions. But the valid actions are still listed, so maybe I can still act. Let me try to take an action to see if the game continues.
Actually, looking at the current state, the dark gray is at [18,15]-[29,29] and the yellow L is at [42,3]-[53,17]. The dark gray is above the yellow L. In Level 1, the solution was LEFT 16, which moved the dark gray to the yellow L's position.
But the game is over, so I can't take any more actions. Let me check if the level was completed or if the game failed.
The output says "Game over? True" and "Level completed? False". This means the game failed, not that the level was completed.
I think the game failed because I took too many actions or because the dark gray character was consumed. I need to restart the level or find a different approach.
But wait, the valid actions are still listed, so maybe I can still act. Let me try to take an action to see if the game continues.
Actually, I think the game is over and I can't take any more actions. The level wasn't completed, so I failed Level 2.
Let me reconsider the problem. In Level 1, the solution was LEFT 16. In Level 2, maybe the solution is different. Let me think about what the goal might be.
Looking at the current state:
- Dark gray at [18,15]-[29,29] (top left)
- Black at [18,45]-[29,59] (top right)
- Yellow L at [42,3]-[53,17] (bottom left)
The dark gray is at [18,15]-[29,29], which is above the yellow L at [42,3]-[53,17]. To move the dark gray to the yellow L's position, I need to move it down by 13 rows (from row 18 to row 42) and left by 12 cols (from col 15 to col 3).
In Level 1, the solution was LEFT 16, which moved the dark gray left 16 times, wrapping around, and eventually reached the yellow L's position. In Level 2, maybe the solution is DOWN 9 + LEFT 4, or some other sequence.
But the game is over, so I can't take any more actions. I need to wait for the next turn or restart the level.
Actually, wait. The user said "The game is over" but the valid actions are still listed. Maybe the game isn't completely over; maybe it's just a level failure, and I need to try a different approach.
Let me try to take an action to see if the game continues. If the game is really over, the action won't do anything.

sections.label: ASSISTANT
sections.content: Game over=True, level_completed=False. Current state shows dark gray at [18,15]-[29,29], black at [18,45]-[29,59], yellow L at [42,3]-[53,17]. The dark gray and black swapped sides. Let me try ACTION7 to see if it resets or continues.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Try ACTION7 to see if it resets or continues
action(['ACTION7'])

seg = current_frame.segmentation
print("After ACTION7:")
for n in seg['nodes']:
    if n['pixels'] > 10 and n['color'] in ['c', 'B', 'Y']:
        b = n['boundary']
        rows = [p[0] for p in b]
        cols = [p[1] for p in b]
        print(f"  {n['color']}: pixels={n['pixels']}, bbox=[{min(rows)},{min(cols)}]-[{max(rows)},{max(cols)}]")
print("Level completed?", last_action_result.get('level_completed'))
print("Game over?", last_action_result.get('game_over'))
print("Step:", current_frame.step)
print("Level:", current_frame.level)
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>
